perturbation.original_text: 2.0 RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of two years from the date of receipt of the information, it shall not disclose or duplicate any information it receives from OWNER that is marked or designated as CONFIDENTIAL, PROPRIETARY or STRICTLY PRIVATE (hereinafter collectively referred to as "PROPRIETARY") to any other person (including RECIPIENT'S employees in any other Group or entity), firm, or company, or use it for its own benefit, except as provided herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.0 RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of two years from the date of receipt of the information, it shall not disclose or duplicate any information it receives from OWNER that is marked or designated as CONFIDENTIAL, PROPRIETARY or STRICTLY PRIVATE (hereinafter referred to as "COMPANY INFORMATION") to any other person (including RECIPIENT'S employees in any other Group or entity), firm, or company, or use it for its own benefit, except as provided herein.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "PROPRIETARY" is changed to "COMPANY INFORMATION", potentially broadening the scope of confidential information beyond what is legally considered proprietary, creating ambiguity and possible conflict with trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification redefines 'PROPRIETARY' as 'COMPANY INFORMATION'. This is problematic because the legal definition of a trade secret (protected under laws like the Defend Trade Secrets Act) requires specific criteria to be met (e.g., reasonable measures to keep the information secret, independent economic value from not being generally known). By broadening the term to include any 'COMPANY INFORMATION,' the contract potentially covers information that doesn't meet the legal threshold for trade secret protection, leading to uncertain enforcement and possibly chilling legitimate competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The change from 'PROPRIETARY' to 'COMPANY INFORMATION' broadens the scope of protected information beyond legally defined trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, this broader definition may encompass information that doesn't meet the legal requirements for trade secret protection, particularly concerning 'reasonable measures to keep the information secret' and 'independent economic value'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' under 18 U.S. Code § 1839, explicitly stating that it includes information where '(A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value...'. By redefining 'PROPRIETARY' as 'COMPANY INFORMATION', the contract potentially covers data that doesn't meet these criteria, directly contradicting the legal definition and therefore creating a legal contradiction by misaligning terminology.

perturbation.original_text: 5.0 The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY and RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to, any such information which; i is already known to RECIPIENT; ii is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of RECIPIENT or any employee or RECIPIENT; (iii is rightfully received from a third party without restriction and without breach of this Agreement; iv is independently developed by RECIPIENT without breach of this Agreement; v is furnished to a third party by OWNER without a similar restriction on third party's rights; vi is approved for release by written authorisation of OWNER; viii. is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a Governmental agency or where disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.0 The parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed PROPRIETARY and RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to, any such information which; i is already known to RECIPIENT; ii is or becomes publicly known through no act of RECIPIENT; (iii is rightfully received from a third party without restriction and without breach of this Agreement; iv is independently developed by RECIPIENT without breach of this Agreement; v is furnished to a third party by OWNER without a similar restriction on third party's rights; vi is approved for release by written authorisation of OWNER; viii. is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a Governmental agency or where disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'or any employee or RECIPIENT' from condition (ii) significantly changes the scope of exception. It implies that even if an employee of the RECIPIENT causes the information to become public, the RECIPIENT still has an obligation. This contradicts the standard understanding of trade secret misappropriation, which generally focuses on the actions of the recipient organization itself.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation in terms of acquiring or disclosing a trade secret *knowing* or *having reason to know* that the trade secret was acquired by improper means, or disclosing or using a trade secret without express or implied consent, by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret, or who at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was derived through improper means. By removing 'or any employee or RECIPIENT' from the clause specifying conditions under which information is not considered proprietary, the modified contract suggests that the RECIPIENT remains liable even if an employee, acting independently and without the RECIPIENT's knowledge or consent, wrongfully discloses the information. This creates a stricter standard of liability than the UTSA, potentially making the contract unenforceable to that extent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the principles of trade secret misappropriation as it broadens the recipient's liability beyond what is typically understood under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). The original text excludes liability when an employee of the recipient independently causes the information to become public, while the changed text removes this exception, potentially holding the recipient liable even for unauthorized actions of their employees. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the UTSA, indicates that the act aims to resolve inconsistencies in trade secret treatment across states and provides definitions for trade secret misappropriation. By removing the phrase 'or any employee or RECIPIENT,' the modified text imposes a stricter liability standard than contemplated by the UTSA, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 13.0 All disputes which cannot be resolved between the parties are to be decided exclusively by the Arbitration Court of the International Chamber of Commerce in London under the arbitration rules of this Chamber.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.0 All disputes which cannot be resolved between the parties are to be decided exclusively by the Arbitration Court of the International Chamber of Commerce in London under rules unilaterally determined by OWNER.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "the arbitration rules of this Chamber" to "rules unilaterally determined by OWNER" makes the arbitration clause unconscionable and likely unenforceable, violating the principle of fair arbitration.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) generally favors the enforcement of arbitration agreements. However, courts may refuse to enforce arbitration agreements that are deemed unconscionable or contrary to public policy. By stipulating that the arbitration rules are unilaterally determined by the OWNER, the modified contract introduces a significant element of unfairness. It gives the OWNER the power to dictate the rules of the arbitration process, potentially biasing the proceedings in their favor. This undermines the fundamental principle of arbitration as a fair and impartial dispute resolution mechanism, conflicting with the spirit and intent of the FAA and general contract law principles of fairness and good faith.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the principle of fair arbitration, which is implied in the FAA's enforcement of arbitration agreements. By granting the OWNER unilateral control over arbitration rules, the modified contract creates a biased process. This undermines the validity of the arbitration agreement as it is no longer a neutral dispute resolution mechanism. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that agreements to arbitrate shall be valid and enforceable 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract'. Unconscionability, due to the unfairness of unilaterally determined rules, is a valid ground for revocation under general contract law principles.

perturbation.original_text: 4.0 RECIPIENT agrees that it will not advise others that, the subject matter of this disclosure is known to OWNER or to others associated with OWNER. RECIPIENT furthermore agrees to exercise prudence in order to avoid the unauthorized disclosure or use, of such information, taking steps which include, but are not limited to, those delineated above.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.0 RECIPIENT agrees that it will actively mislead others into believing that the subject matter of this disclosure is unknown to OWNER or to others associated with OWNER. RECIPIENT furthermore agrees to exercise prudence in order to avoid the unauthorized disclosure or use, of such information, taking steps which include, but are not limited to, those delineated above.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not advise' to 'will actively mislead' now compels the recipient to take active measures that may constitute fraud or misrepresentation, which is illegal under most legal systems, particularly regarding commercial interactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, which states 'RECIPIENT agrees that it will actively mislead others into believing that the subject matter of this disclosure is unknown to OWNER or to others associated with OWNER,' directly contradicts fraud and misrepresentation laws. These laws, including 18 U.S. Code § 1341 (Mail Fraud), prohibit schemes or artifices to defraud or obtain money or property by means of false or fraudulent pretenses. While this specific statute addresses mail fraud, the general principle is that intentionally misleading others for commercial gain is illegal. The modification compels the RECIPIENT to engage in conduct that would be considered a misrepresentation of facts, thereby potentially violating these laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1341 - Frauds and swindles | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1341 - Frauds and swindles U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever, having devised or intending to devise any scheme or artifice to defraud , or for obtaining money or property by means of false or fraudulent pretenses, representations, or promises, or to sell, dispose of, loan, exchange, alter, give away, distribute, supply, or furnish or procure for unlawful use any counterfeit or spurious coin, obligation, security, or other article, or anything represented to be or intimated or held out to be such counterfeit or spurious article, for the purpose of executing such scheme or artifice or attempting so to do, places in any post office or authorized depository for mail matter, any matter or thing whatever to be sent or delivered by the Postal Service, or deposits or causes to be deposited any matter or thing whatever to be sent or delivered by any private or commercial interstate carrier, or takes or receives therefrom, any such matter or thing, or knowingly causes to be delivered by mail or such carrier according to the direction thereon, or at the place at which it is directed to be delivered by the person to whom it is addressed, any such matter or thing, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both. If the violation occurs in relation to, or involving any benefit authorized, transported, transmitted, transferred, disbursed, or paid in connection with, a presidentially declared major disaster or emergency (as those terms are defined in section 102 of the Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act ( 42 U.S.C. 5122 )), or affects a financial institution, such person shall be fined not more than $1,000,000 or imprisoned not more than 30 years, or both. (June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 763 ; May 24, 1949, ch. 139, § 34, 63 Stat. 94 ;
perturbation.justification: The modified text compels the RECIPIENT to actively mislead others, which directly contradicts laws against fraud and misrepresentation. The original text prohibited advising, while the changed text mandates actively misleading, which is a significant and direct contradiction. 18 U.S. Code § 1341, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits 'any scheme or artifice to defraud, or for obtaining money or property by means of false or fraudulent pretenses, representations, or promises.' The changed text creates a situation where the RECIPIENT is obligated to engage in such fraudulent pretenses, thus violating this statute.
